I'm saying I built out six to eight months worth of marketing automation for Grant Cardone, for his Cardone University and then again for his Cardone on Demand system. I'm saying I trained Grant Cardone's entire sales team over 25 salespeople on how to potentially follow up using Infusionsoft and I optimized the entire Infusionsoft campaign, growing it from 150,000 people in a database to 453,000 people. I've since sent about 23 million emails a month and we've generated tens of millions of dollars in gross returns for our clients selling tangible and intangible information products. Don't do anything that takes away from the overall amount of money that you can accumulate the profit that you can put away. Build a team. Hire a team. Start off with the graphic designer. The graphic designer should have the multi hat position to also be somebody who is a master at click bumps. They should be able to create social graphics. Now if you're a social media marketing agency you're obviously going to want to hire a social media manager, a social media specialist, a social media manager, whatever you want to call it. Now this social media manager position is good because you can immediately go and sell this position. You can go and sell social services as soon as you have these two roles covered. You can also sell social services if you only have this one role and you outsource graphic design. I tell you to bring these two things in house for control purposes. I've spent tens of thousands of dollars on business consultants who have built and sold several eight-figure businesses. We've taught me time and time again that insourcing and having everybody hired gives you more control, which gives you more potential for influence and long-term abilities to deliver. Your speed to delivery is important as we spoke about in the last video. These two positions are great to hire as your first two positions in business. I highly recommend either adding a project manager, and I'm going to explain why, or I recommend bringing in another position that can drive you a return on investment. Now I want you to remember that if you'd like it anytime you can bring in what I call a sales and marketing manager, and the sales and marketing manager is somebody who you duplicate yourself into to go out and generate leads in business. My sales and marketing managers today have not done a great job at closing, but they've done the best job at generating appointments, scheduling those appointments, having me coming on the phone with reset people who are already in my calendar, who already know who I am, who I've become hyped up to, where I come in and close them up every time. Now besides this, commonly people say like, oh, I need an assistant or something. That's incredible to have. And that's a great hire. I personally find that what's called an executive assistant is worth more than anything else. I'm also going to give you guys an alternative or price here, depending on the level that you guys may want to operate at. That being said, this executive assistant position is somebody who needs to be literally a smaller version of you. Cycles can range from small to large to obviously mid-range cycles additionally, meaning you can have things like you need to do your laundry, you need to put clean tickets, or you need to push your calendar back, or you need to go schedule that barber appointment all the way up to things where you have to go out and do an in-person consultation and spend nine hours of your day there delivering an in-person consult. That being said, smaller cycles get in your way as you perform and keep on growing the amount of cycles that you're a part of and the size of the cycles overall. An executive assistant, when you have large enough cycles in enough consistency to where the smaller cycles officially bog you down and are getting done, can be a great position to add in to free up some stress and help alleviate areas of your personal life. Executive assistant also needs to be able to duplicate smaller versions of yourself that relate to business. Meaning you need to be able to go and talk to your team members. You need to be able to go and talk to clients in a confident way to be able to articulate that your calendar is being pushed back or that you need to have a conversation with so and so inside of the team, whatever. Your executive assistant needs to be a smaller version of you. You can also depend on the amount of revenue that you have inside of your business. Maybe you need an executive assistant sooner than later because you know that all of your small cycles are holding your back no matter what level you're on in business. A great service you can use right away, doesn't matter what level you're on, I would actually recommend signing up for it. Literally right now, it's called MAGIC. MAGIC is a personal assistant service that's through text messaging. So you get this little five digit number that they text you from. My girl's named Molly and I text Molly anytime of the day and she does anything that I ever tell her to do ever and I actually feel better about saying it in most cases to her than I do to my executive assistant. My executive assistant I pay $3,000 a month for. MAGIC I pay about $200 to $400 a month for it. It's $35 an hour, they charge by the minute. Executive assistant tax done by MAGIC typically take less than 5 to 15 minutes. Shit. The best service I'm thinking about firing my executive assistant except for the small things I have my executive assistant do that MAGIC can't do because it's not a real in-person. This isn't a bot. This isn't a chat thing. It's a real individual. That being said, I highly recommend if you're like me, long term about three or four in my business. The amount that I would pay each one of these people on the roll into inside of the payroll section, just to give you an idea, when I first bring in anybody, it doesn't matter who they are or who they think they are, I immediately do tests. For the first week, I typically do about a $500 test up to about a $750 test in some cases. Once again, depending on who they are and who they think they are. $500 to $750 is about what I want to pay them on a week to week basis anyway. For the work that I want them to do, sometimes less, sometimes more depending on where I'm at in business and what I want them to do and the level of expertise that I want them to already have coming into my company. I've found that any of these positions are people who already have expertise already do their job well, but just looking for that extra mentorship and that extra little rigging wrong, great work environment, good company culture to thrive in in order to get to the next levels of themselves. I personally am confident that I have that kind of system and structure within my company, so I am comfortable and confident taking on people who might be a little shelled up at the time when they come in, who I know I can bust right out of that show and get them on to the next levels of themselves. That being said, I then get more dedication, more loyalty and overall more productivity from those types of people than the people who are already experts that are well established who have that cocky persona that nobody likes. All that being said, in week number one, I'm saying, hey, I want you to come in and do all this stuff, so I'm overwhelming the shit out of them in week one, saying, do this, do this, do this, do this, do this. I want it done in a high quality and I want you to ask me all questions. I'm testing the hell out of people in week number one if they get into my business. I talk more about the interviews than I do, but just so you guys know, I always, always before I hire anybody, put them through a severe interview process and an IQ, EQ and aptitude test to make sure that they're up to par. That being said, after they pass week one, I might also do that in week number two, dependent on where they are at financially. Some people come to you out of school, they just deliver their parents, they don't need as much money as other people, they can do these little tests and they're okay with it. They come to you and they're like, hey, I want to intern. That means, hey, I want to work for you and I want to get mentorship, but I don't want to be paid a lot. So I do that too. That being said, after you've gotten past that little one to two week period, you need to find your payroll. Most people at the lowest end in my business have about a $2,500 payroll. Some of them have up to a $4,500 payroll depending on their position and overall bonuses that they generate. If somebody is on commission in my business and they're generating 20% of overall sales for that they're putting into my business, we in my company do a minimum of $50,000. We do a $15,000 up to a $100,000 deal. We actually sell them too, which is sweet, unlike some people who just say they have those minimums. We in business give 20% of those sales to our salespeople as they bring them in. So the highest paid people in my company are commonly my sales and marketing image. I had a guy make $9,000 a month, compared to everybody else, all because he did, I think, two deals or three deals or something like that, accumulated some great commissions. That being said, this is typically on a monthly basis. So the revenue that we're paying comes and we'll go more into this on the payroll section on the fifth and on the 20th. So I pay out my payroll on the fifth and on the 20th, which is about bi-weekly. And being said, if I'm paying somebody $2,500 or $4,500 or whatever I am in between, it's just that number divided by two. So half of it's paid on the fifth and half of it's paid on the 20th. There's no janky stuff to it. This is the dream team. This is the perfect team that you can lay out.
